body_txt: The " Yellow Peril " was an era of heightened racial paranoia in Europe and the Americas toward Asia in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Asians were viewed as morally bankrupt, their cultures as rejecting and hating Western values, and their society as an existential threat to the West and the white race as a whole. While xenophobia toward people from far-off lands was nothing new, and European societies had long seen things through the lens of The West vs. The Far East , it was kicked into overdrive during the 19th century for several reasons. Burgeoning international trade networks made the world smaller and Asia far less distant. Many East Asian countries were antagonistic to the European powers (oftentimes with good reason—see The Opium Wars ), making it easy for the white public to accept narratives of Asians being naturally hostile to the West. Large numbers of Chinese people began immigrating to countries like the United States, Canada, and Australia, where their racial and cultural differences, ethnic enclaves , and competition for jobs made them visible targets for white resentment. The science of racism was also becoming extensively codified, and the perceived physical and moral inferiority of nonwhite groups was largely accepted as incontrovertible scientific truth. These factors all came together to form a narrative that Asians weren't simply bad, they were downright dangerous to white society. Their people are criminals who only seek to profit at other races' expense (and probably plot to rape white women , too). Their leaders are tyrannical despots who want to destroy the West through underhanded means. And as a whole race, their goal is nothing less than driving white people to extinction! Polemics about the threat of " yellow hordes " overrunning the West graced novels and newspapers. Laws to limit or ban Asian immigration, like the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 , were passed in the U.S. and Canada. The vitriol was ratcheted up even further whenever Western countries went to war with Asian ones, such as China in the Boxer Rebellion or Japan in World War II . The cultural impact of the Yellow Peril is best seen through the media and characters it spawned, almost all of them reinforcing negative stereotypes: Diabolical Mastermind villains (the most enduring of which is Fu Manchu ), sexually and morally suspect Dragon Ladies , opium dealers , Triad gangsters , and so on. The closest to positive representation one could hope for were slit-eyed , buck-toothed , Ching Chong -speaking comic relief characters (probably also played by white actors in yellowface , to boot). Pushback against the stereotype began as early as the 1920s and only intensified from there. Ronald Knox 's "Ten Commandments" of the Fair-Play Whodunnit said that "no Chinaman must figure in the story," a reference to the overuse of Chinese characters as Scary Minority Suspects in mystery stories. Characters like Charlie Chan and Mr. Moto were intelligent Asian protagonists whose creators were trying to avert this trope; while they were often played by white actors in yellowface, they're generally regarded as Fair For Their Day . It's a largely Discredited Trope nowadays, although piecemeal aspects of it may rear their head in modern stories with Asian antagonists. A subtrope of Orientalism . Compare China Takes Over the World , "Great Replacement" Conspiracy Theory , Japan Takes Over the World , and Red Scare . See also Greedy Jew and Where da White Women At? for other racial panics with similar motifs. Note: A work simply having one or more Asian antagonists does not make it a Yellow Peril work. It must have the notion that Asians are mortal enemies of the West or draw on stereotypes those notions created. No Real Life Examples, Please!
